summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – ORDERS – Enforcement – Where an order had previously been made directing the mother to produce the child’s expired passport and sign a passport application – Where the mother did not comply with the order – Where the father seeks an order pursuant to s 11(1)(b)(i) of the Australian Passports Act 2005 (Cth) for a passport to issue notwithstanding absence of consent by mother – Where until further order the child’s name is to remain on the Family Law Watchlist – Where an order is made FAMILY LAW – COSTS – Where an application for costs is made – Where the Application in a Proceeding would not have been necessary other than for the mother’s non-compliance with a previous order of the Court – Where costs are ordered fixed in the amount of $1,611.50

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

CAREW J:

The parenting matter between Mr Ritter (“the father”) and Ms Hermann (“the mother”) was listed for final hearing over five days in December 2025 but concluded at the end of the second day.
The decision was and remains reserved until 27 February 2026.
At the conclusion of the hearing, the father was granted leave to make an oral application for an interim order allowing him to spend time with X (“the child”) before he left Australia to return to his home in City N [in Country D] and for an order that the mother sign a passport application for the child.
The mother opposed the interim order sought by the father that he spend time with the child prior to his departure other than for the possibility of a two hour “play date”. The mother did not oppose signing the passport application and agreed that she could bring in the child’s expired passport.
The following exchange took place between myself and the mother during the mother’s oral submissions at the end of the trial:
...

HER HONOUR: But what about the signing a passport application for her tomorrow?

THE MOTHER: I don’t believe I will have enough time, but I will try.

HER HONOUR: Well, there would be a form.

THE MOTHER: I’ve just got to fill out the form and return it.

HER HONOUR: If there was a form produced, you could sign it, couldn’t you?

THE MOTHER: Yes.

HER HONOUR: Okay. And any reason why you couldn’t produce her expired passport tomorrow?

THE MOTHER: No.

HER HONOUR: Okay, thank you. All right. Anything else you would like to say, [the mother]?

THE MOTHER: No.

...
On 16 December 2025, I made an order permitting the father to spend time with the child from 12.00 pm on 17 December 2025 until 3.00 pm on 19 December 2025. The order required the child be brought into Court Children’s Services so the order could be explained to the child and to facilitate the changeover from the mother to the father.
On 16 December 2025 at 6.36 pm, the father’s solicitor wrote to the mother requesting, in addition to providing the child’s expired passport as ordered, the mother provide the child’s birth certificate, Australian Citizenship Certificate and a copy of the mother’s current or most recent passport to progress a passport application for the child.
The time between the father and the child, as ordered, went ahead.
However, the mother did not sign the passport application nor produce the child’s expired passport as ordered. Several attempts were made by the father’s legal representatives to contact the mother by telephone and email asking her to return to the Court and sign the application, but she did not respond.
On 23 December 2025, the father filed an Application in a Proceeding seeking enforcement of the 16 December 2025 order and additionally seeking that the mother produce other identified documents to progress the passport and visa application for the child in City N, in the event that an order is made for the child to travel to City N after 27 February 2026. The father included some orders in the alternative in the event the mother does not comply with this further order.
Today, the father indicated that he only sought to press the order made pursuant to the Australian Passports Act 2005 (Cth) (“the Act”) that would enable the issue of a passport notwithstanding the mother’s lack of consent. Ms Tasso, solicitor for the father, also indicated that it would be of assistance to receive a copy of the child’s Australian Citizenship Certificate, and the mother has agreed to provide that. The mother also provided during this hearing today the number on the child’s Australian Citizenship Certificate and confirmed the child’s full name that appears on that certificate.
All parties were given leave to appear at today’s hearing via video link. The father lives in City N and his lawyers are in Sydney. The mother contended that she was unable to take time off work today to appear in person but that she could take a break to appear via video. The mother was ordered to file an affidavit from her employer corroborating her evidence. The mother failed to do so.
The mother opposes the order sought by the father, contending that it should either be dismissed or adjourned until after the delivery of judgment in the substantive proceedings on 27 February 2026.
The fact remains that an order was made on 16 December 2025 for the mother to sign a passport application for the child and deliver the child’s expired passport to the father’s solicitor. The mother is in breach of that order. The mother does not seek to excuse her failure to comply with the order. The mother merely objects to the child travelling overseas. No order I make today will authorise the child’s departure from Australia. That is a matter that will be considered in the substantive proceedings. In any event, there remains a Family Law Watchlist order in place preventing the child from leaving Australia so the issue of the passport itself will not enable travel overseas at this time.
There is some urgency surrounding the issue of a passport for the child, in that if the father’s application to live with the child in City N is successful, he proposes to depart Australia on or shortly after 27 February 2026. If the father’s application in the substantive proceedings is unsuccessful it is nevertheless anticipated that the child will travel overseas at some point, whether that be to City N or the United Kingdom or elsewhere, and whether that be with the father or the mother. It is convenient to deal with the application for the issue of the passport order at this time.
It seems to me that the most effective means of securing a passport for the child is to make an order pursuant to s 11(1)(b)(i) of the Act which authorises the issue of a passport notwithstanding the absence of the mother’s consent. I am not persuaded to order the mother produce the other documents initially requested by the father in his application as I have no confidence that the mother will comply with such an order. It seems to me that the father should be able to obtain the other documents he needs upon application to the appropriate department or authority. The order I propose to make and these reasons for judgment should assist the father in that endeavour.
The application today was brought as a direct result of the mother’s failure to comply with the order made by me on 16 December 2025. In those circumstances, it is entirely reasonable that the father apply for and receive the costs associated with him having to file an application and have his solicitor appear today. The mother will pay the father’s costs of and incidental to his application fixed in the sum of $1,611.50 and those costs are to be paid within 14 days.
